text: All right, this is a patient of an elderly woman who has had chronic cholecystitis for several years, extensive cholelithiasis. You can see, again, standard fashion. As I always state before, I use four robot arms, ProGrasp in the left arm, Maryland Dissector in arm number two, camera, and then a hook cotter, as you can see. And the interesting thing about this case is that the ICG that was given in the holding room thirty minutes before didn't light up the cystic duct or the common bile duct. As you can see, I keep toggling back and forth between immunofluorescence imaging, so I'm trying to get an idea because the cystic duct appears to be somewhat very dilated, but also I thought it may just be a very long infundibulum.

The surgeon uses robotic instruments to carefully manipulate and tease the cystic duct, attempting to achieve the critical view of safety. Despite toggling between standard and immunofluorescence imaging, the cystic duct and biliary tree do not illuminate with indocyanine green. The surgeon performs short cautery applications on crossing vessels and continues to milk the duct, observing no stones present.
The surgeon isolates the cystic artery, visually inspecting for stones but finding none.
The surgeon uses round tip scissors without an energy attachment to carefully dissect the tissue from the cystic plate.
Nothing stressful, nothing fancy, just wanted to show how the indesign and green didn't really work in an elective case.
